title: Hack@DAC 2021: AES CTR counter never increments
tagline: One missing +1 turns AES into a fixed keystream.
hook_30s: AES in counter mode requires a counter that increments every block. If it doesn't, your cipher becomes a one-time-pad with a fixed keystream — a known-plaintext attack recovers your secrets. This bug was inserted in a Hack@DAC SoC. ChipGPT finds it in 8 seconds.

proof.note: Open-source formal (SymbiYosys) proves it via the combinational next-state form — ctr_d must differ from ctr_q under the increment condition — and a 2-block directed test reproduces it. The textbook temporal $past form would need commercial SVA.

steps.agent: Task: verify the AES CTR-mode counter in aes_ctr.sv against the NIST CTR specification.
Reading NIST SP 800-38A CTR mode. Extracting the counter rule.
Structured fact: successive counter blocks must differ; each block increments the counter. Failure mode is severe — fixed keystream.
Parsing aes_ctr.sv. Locating the next-state logic that drives the counter register ctr_q.

BUG. The next-counter value is ctr_d = ctr_q with no increment on any path. mode_ctr_i and block_done_i are inputs but never gate an increment. The counter is static: every block uses keystream block 0. CWE-1240.

artifact.explanation: After a block completes in CTR mode, the counter must change.
artifact.sva:
assert property (@(posedge clk_i) disable iff (!rst_ni)
  (mode_ctr_i && block_done_i) |=> (ctr_o != $past(ctr_o)));
artifact.result: Formal (SymbiYosys z3): counterexample found via the combinational form. Sim: fails on block 2.

steps.agent: Directed test: CTR mode, encrypt two consecutive blocks, compare counter values.

artifact.name: t_ctr_increments_per_block
artifact.stimulus: mode_ctr_i=1; pulse block_done_i; sample ctr_o across two blocks.

steps.agent: Confirmed: AES CTR counter never increments.

artifact.title: AES CTR counter never increments
artifact.root_cause: Next-counter logic holds ctr_q; per-block increment clause absent.
artifact.impact: CTR degenerates to a fixed-keystream one-time-pad; keystream recoverable via known plaintext.
